ये 3 गलतियाँ आपको कर सकती हैं कंगाल! 

क्या आप भी अनजाने में रात के समय ये काम कर रहे हैं? ज्योतिष शास्त्र और वास्तु के अनुसार, रात में कुछ काम करने से घर में नकारात्मक ऊर्जा (negative energy) आती है और माँ लक्ष्मी नाराज़ हो जाती हैं, जिससे गरीबी और कंगाली का सामना करना पड़ सकता है। 

भूलकर भी रात में न करें ये 3 काम:
1️⃣ रात में झाड़ू लगाना: ऐसा करने से घर की बरकत चली जाती है।
2️⃣ जूठे बर्तन छोड़ना: किचन में रातभर जूठे बर्तन रखने से राहु-केतु का दोष बढ़ता है और धन हानि होती है।
3️⃣ बाल या नाखून काटना: सूर्यास्त के बाद बाल या नाखून काटने से सेहत और समृद्धि दोनों पर बुरा असर पड़ता है।

For many Number 4 people, 2026 (up to February) is bringing opportunities, growth, and a strong prosperity phase.

I’ve had so many Number 4 people tell me that suddenly new opportunities appeared, career doors opened, finances improved, or things finally started moving after feeling stuck for a long time.

But every now and then, someone says:

“I can’t relate to this at all.”

And that’s where the bigger picture comes in.

Your birth number is important, but it is not the only thing that influences your results.

Your name vibration matters.
Your numerology chart matters.

Think of it this way.

A powerful prosperity yog is like being gifted the blueprint for a beautiful building.

But if the foundation underneath is weak or incomplete, it becomes difficult to fully benefit from what’s available to you.

An imbalanced chart can sometimes weaken the support systems needed to make the most of favourable periods and opportunities.

That’s why looking at only one number is never enough.

A complete Numerology Chart Analysis helps reveal where your strengths are, what may be creating delays, and what can be improved for better alignment.

Number 4s… does this feel familiar?

No matter how hard you work, it can feel like you’re constantly swimming against the current. Hustle, struggle, grind… putting in more effort than everyone else just to stay afloat.

And then there’s the overgiving.
Doing too much. Giving too much. Showing up for everyone.

But somehow… it feels like a one-way road.
You pour in your time, energy, effort, love… and very little comes back.

This is where many Number 4s get stuck.

Your strength is your willingness to work hard and show up. But when that turns into overdoing, overgiving, and ignoring your own limits, life has a way of forcing boundaries for you. Sometimes through endings. Sometimes through distance. Sometimes by removing people or situations altogether.

The lesson for Number 4 is not to stop caring.
It’s to care with boundaries.
To give with awareness.
To stop treating exhaustion like a badge of honour.

If you’re a Number 4, your breakthrough may not come from doing more. It may come from doing differently.

“If Number 1 is the piercing light of the Sun, Number 2 is the gentle, intuitive pull of the Moon. Your sensitivity isn’t a weakness; it is your ultimate superpower.” 🌙🔮✨

In numerology, Number 2 is ruled by the Moon. It represents duality, collaboration, emotional depth, and receptivity. Unlike the aggressive, forward-driving energy of a Number 1, people aligned with Number 2 hold the world together through empathy, intuition, and a profound creative flow. here’s a lil secret. (my personal belief) the more you heal, regulate your nervous system, heal from past traumas, the more beautiful you become. Now I’m not calling myself beautiful, because it doesn’t feel right to me. But I FEEL beautiful which makes me want to take care of myself externally. In fact, everything kind of just aligns on its own when you’re operating from a healed place. EVERYTHING comes from within. It’s that inner glow that only healing produces 😇 for more context, I have severely struggled with body dysmorphia, weight fluctuations (not just cute lil on and off 5 pounds but the depre$$ion kind of massive weight gain a few times in my life), many eating disorders, starvation, unnecessarily beating myself up at the gym with cardio, etc. you get the point!!! Now I’m not writing this like oooo look at me, hard life, feel sorry for me blah blah. I’m just getting my point across very clearly. It’s a miserable way of living, constantly thinking about your weight, food, imperfections, etc. it’s sooooo useless and pointless. Your brain is used to consistent patterns which revolves around topics like food and weight. Now it’s easier said than done (obviously) but it’s truly up to YOU to change something about it. I genuinely believe it’s as simple as making a conscious decision to WANT to change your life for the better. What other people think doesn’t matter. Who cares? Social media is fake. A picture is barely a second of someone’s life. Also none of that FAKE social media healing crap either 💀 real healing isn’t always pretty. And like, everyone is on their own personal journey. You really don’t need confirmation or validation from someone else. We all have intuition (some are more tapped in than others) and what im trying to say is that we ALWAYS, and I mean always know what we have to do, not do, remove, etc to better our lives. we always know internally. It’s just that the body unfortunately keeps score 🥹 especially if you have complex ptsd as an adult, it’s really hard to physically keep up with your mind. The body is still recovering from trauma. Just be kind to yourself and others ❤️‍🩹 you deserve to have a happy brain☺️

Get ready for Earth joy!

Earlier today, we announced the four astronauts who will go to space as part of Artemis III. The four crew members are:

- NASA astronaut Randy Bresnik (@astrokomrade)
- ESA (@EuropeanSpaceAgency) astronaut Luca Parmitano (@astro_luca)
- NASA astronaut Andre Douglas (@astro_andred)
- NASA astronaut Frank Rubio (@astro_frankrubio)

As part of the Artemis III mission, these four astronauts will undertake a series of challenging tests in low Earth orbit in 2027, setting future @NASAArtemis missions up to return humanity to the Moon.

Check out the link in bio to learn more about Artemis III!

Credit: NASA
#NASA #Artemis #ICYMI

If the sky is pink and white… 🎵⁣
⁣
@NASAChandraXray and @NASAWebb came together to give us this stunningly pink image of Westerlund 2, a giant star cluster. The stars in this image are between one and three million years old.⁣
⁣
Data from the two telescopes has revealed not only newborn stars, but brown dwarfs – objects that are bigger than planets, but smaller than stars – and swirls of gas.⁣
⁣
#NASA #Stars #Space #Galaxy

A view earned only by leaving the world that made us.

From low Earth orbit, the planet reveals itself in motion — aurora sweeping across the night side, the atmosphere glowing in thin, electric layers, and pieces of the International Space Station drifting quietly through view. In this timelapse, astronauts capture the rhythm of Earth as only orbit can show it: fast, fluid, and alive.

Every frame is a reminder of why we study our home planet from above — to understand its systems, protect its future, and witness the beauty that unfolds just beyond the horizon.

Introducing Artemis III.

Four astronauts. Three launches. Two dockings. One splashdown.

In 2027, the Artemis III mission will practice docking the Orion spacecraft with two lunar landers in low Earth orbit. On future Artemis missions, landers will bring astronauts to the lunar surface. While Artemis III will not land on the Moon, it will test the complex capabilities we need to return humanity there — this time to stay.

NASA astronauts @AstroKomrade, @Astro_FrankRubio, and @Astro_AndreD will join @EuropeanSpaceAgency astronaut @Astro_Luca on this historic mission.

Credit: NASA

#Artemis #ArtemisIII #Astronauts

An image that feels like summer ☀️

Beyond the Orion Nebula is a long and massive filament of cold gas and dust, divided into four parts and collectively called the Orion Molecular Clouds. This image, captured by @NASAWebb, shows just a small portion of one of the clouds.

Every stage of star formation — from the youngest stellar embryos, to protoplanetary discs, to newly-minted pre-main sequence stars — is contained within this scene (captured by Webb’s Near-Infared Camera) which stretches 150 light-years across. 

The intense star-forming activity has produced an impressive display of billowing outflows and sparkling stars atop swirling layers of gas and dark, obscuring clouds. Only in the infrared do we see protostars begin to shine out from the cocoons of dust in which they are born.

Read more at the link in @ESAWebb’s bio.

Coming soon: one of history’s most complex missions 

Tune into our Instagram Live on Tuesday, June 9, at 11:30 a.m. EDT (1530 UTC) to meet the astronauts flying aboard Artemis III, the mission that will test rendezvous and docking capabilities with commercial landers in low Earth orbit. Commercial landers are needed to bring astronauts to the lunar surface during future Artemis missions. 

Before Artemis IV heads back to the Moon, this third mission will be an important stepping stone for lunar landings during future Artemis missions. 

Credit: NASA

#Artemis #Astronaut
There is a lot going on right now on the @iss, but fortunately we are all safe and witnessed a spectacular southern aurora show yesterday thanks to a recent solar event.

Journey to the center of the galaxy cluster

This spiral galaxy, known as Messier 88 or M88, can be found 63 million light-years from Earth in the Virgo Cluster, a collection of more than 1,000 galaxies held together by gravity. 

As its galaxies swirl and mix, M88 will spend hundreds of millions of years slowly drifting toward the innermost reaches of the Virgo Cluster. Along the way, it will get pushed and pulled by other galaxies in the cluster — reshaping it forever on its cosmic journey.

#NASA #Galaxy #TravelAddict

You’re not unc. You’re not chopped. You’re a vibrant, sparkly cosmic squid 🦑

This dazzling galaxy goes by many names: NGC 1068, Messier 77, and the Squid Galaxy. It’s located about 45 million light-years away from Earth, and can be seen from the ground with a small telescope in the constellation Cetus, or the Whale.

Like our own Milky Way, the Squid Galaxy has a black hole at its center. However, its black hole is twice as massive as our galaxy's black hole and about 15 million times the mass of our Sun.

This image is a combination of views from multiple telescopes, including @NASAChandraXray, @NASAHubble, and @NASAWebb.

#NASA #Galaxy #Universe

Everything's a matter of perspective 🌏

If you ever look up and see the International Space Station orbiting Earth, the astronauts aboard might be looking back up at you! Here, NASA astronaut @astro_hathaway and @europeanspaceagency astronaut Sophie Adenot are looking through the @ISS's cupola window at the Indian Ocean. This photo was taken on May 17, 2026.

Check out our link in bio to see when the space station will pass over your hometown!

#NASA #Space #Earth

I’m so hungry, I could eat two million Suns!

In this photo taken by @NASAChandraXray and @NASAWebb, a supermassive black hole at the center of a spiral galaxy is feasting on its surroundings. This black hole is about 60 million light-years from Earth and has a mass of two million Suns—and it’s only growing larger.

With supermassive black holes like this, scientists have wondered which comes first: the galaxy or the black hole? New data from Webb may help to explain how some black holes can grow so big without a larger galaxy to feed them. Check our link in bio for more details!

#NASA #blackhole #galaxy

A “starry” galaxy far, far away
 
Peek behind the bright star at the center of this image and you’ll see what appears to be a swarm of faint stars. This is actually a dwarf irregular galaxy residing some 23 million light-years away in the constellation Canis Major. (“Dwarf irregular” means this galaxy has an unusual shape. It’s not a spiral, round, oval, or lenticular – kind of a cross between a spiral and oval.)
 
This image comes from a @NASAHubble program that looked at the movement of galaxies and galaxy clusters through space. Matter in the universe is distributed unevenly, and the gravitational influence that matter has drives the movement of large-scale structures in the universe.
 
#NASA #Hubble #Space #Galaxy

Demonstrating VIPER's path from lander to lunar surface 🌕

We completed a critical demonstration of our offloader mechanism, using rotating arms and cables to safely lower a VIPER mass simulator from the top of Blue Moon MK1's forward module to the lunar surface.

Key achievements included:
✅ Full deployment sequence with davit arms under load
✅ Winch cable system maintaining robust control throughout the full lowering sequence
✅ Performance across roll and pitch slopes up to 10°
✅ Clearances and mechanical interfaces

Next up: critical design review and further development testing.

📍 Lunar Plant 1 

Testing in Florida continues for Endurance. Radio frequency compatibility testing is on deck.

Endurance is going through a variety of tests ahead of launch in Florida. First one complete: modal testing ✅ 

We used sensors to measure how our lunar lander will respond to launch conditions. The test was performed with Endurance mounted on both the New Glenn flight Launch Vehicle Separation System (LVSS) and a GS2 payload adapter.
BE-7 Flight Engine 3 has completed hotfire testing, verifying engine performance prior to flight. Next, it will head to Lunar Plant 1 to join the lunar lander engine fleet.
Welcome back to the Space Coast, again, "Never Tell Me The Odds."

Endurance is back on the Space Coast in Florida!

Up next: testing MK1’s launch vehicle separation system and communications system, and performing a wet dress rehearsal of the lander, where we fully fuel with cryogenic propellants. Once complete, we will encapsulate it in New Glenn’s fairing and prepare for launch.

Here's an inside look at one of the many tests we conducted during Endurance's thermal vacuum chamber (TVAC) testing — the first-ever deployment of its high-gain antenna in the demanding temperature- and vacuum-environment of space. This antenna system will provide high-rate telemetry and video during flight and from the Moon's South Pole. Thank you to the team at @nasajohnson and throughout @NASA for the collaboration during these tests. Endurance is now arriving to the Space Coast!

This iconic image, which would come to be known as the Pale Blue Dot, was taken by Voyager 1 on Valentine’s Day 1990.

The spacecraft, which had launched in 1977, was speeding out of the solar system — beyond Neptune and about 3.7 billion miles (6 billion kilometers) from the Sun — when mission managers commanded it to look back toward home one final time.

In it, the Earth appears as a single pixel in a dark expanse — small, fragile, and irreplaceable.

After snapping the Pale Blue Dot and other “family photos,” Voyager 1 powered off its cameras forever.

Can you think of another single image that has inspired as much love for our home planet?

This flyby used a gravity assist from the planet to provide a critical boost in speed and to adjust the Psyche spacecraft’s path, all without using any onboard propellant. This maneuver sent the spacecraft on the final part of its journey to its target: the mysterious, metal-rich asteroid Psyche.

The successful Mars flyby also provided the mission team with a valuable practice run ahead of arrival at the asteroid in 2029.

The ground beneath Mexico City is slowly sinking, and now, the NISAR satellite can track it from space. 

New data shows parts of the city (in blue) that sank more than half an inch (more than 2 cm) per month from Oct. 2025 to Jan. 2026. The findings are not surprising because Mexico City is already known as one of the fastest-sinking capitals in the world, but they do show how quickly and reliably NISAR can track changes across Earth’s surface. Several generations of space-based radar have tracked Mexico City, and NISAR is now advancing these efforts.

This flyer from 1911, advertising a fair and carnival in Rutland, Vermont, that promises “aeroplane flights every day” captures the public excitement surrounding early aviation. The first air races, meets, and exhibitions kicked off a wave of public enthusiasm for aviation that circled the globe. Aerial exhibitions and meets introduced the airplane to the public in truly thrilling ways. People flocked to these events, anxious to see daring aviators show their stuff. Still more devoured newspaper accounts of the latest aerial derring-do. The men and women who flew ever higher, faster, and farther emerged as the great heroes of the era. The stage was set for a new age shaped by the new reality of human flight.

This flyer is the artifact from Vermont for our "50 for 50" digital project celebrating the 50th anniversary of the Museum and the U.S. 250th. We will be highlighting one artifact from each U.S. state and territory every day until our anniversary on July 1.

The Sikorsky JRS-1 is the only aircraft in the Museum that was at Pearl Harbor, Hawaii, on December 7, 1941. After the JRS-1 survived the attack undamaged, Wesley Hoyt “Wes” Ruth flew the unarmed aircraft for about 250 miles searching for the Japanese fleet, with only some sailors armed with bolt action rifles for protection. Ruth was awarded the Navy Cross for his actions that day. The JRS-1 is currently in the Mary Baker Engen Restoration Hangar at the Museum’s Steven F. Udvar-Hazy Center undergoing a restoration project.

The jRS-1 is the artifact from Hawaii for our "50 for 50" digital project celebrating the 50th anniversary of the Museum and the U.S. 250th. We will be highlighting one artifact from each U.S. state and territory every day until our anniversary on July 1.

The newly-invented integrated circuit, or silicon “chip,” was among the many innovations in the Apollo lunar module’s onboard computer. Astronauts used this computer to guide the LM to a safe landing. Smaller and lighter than glass vacuum tubes, silicon chips allowed the computer to meet the LM’s weight, size, and power requirements. A pre-production silicon integrated circuit of the type used in the Apollo Guidance Computer is on display in Boeing Milestones of Flight Hall at the Museum in DC. The thousands of chips used in the Apollo Guidance Computer were produced by the Philco-Ford company at a plant in Lansdale, Pennsylvania.

Celebrating the 50th anniversary of the Museum and the U.S. 250th, the “50 for 50” project will spotlight one artifact from every U.S. state and territory.

The spacecraft from which Ed White completed the first American spacewalk is in our collection. It carried White and James McDivitt to space and back on the Gemini IV mission, which ended on this day in 1965. It is on display in Boeing Milestones of Flight Hall at the Museum in DC. #AirSpacePhoto

In 1978, Ben Abruzzo, Maxie Anderson, and Larry Newman became the first people to fly non-stop across the Atlantic Ocean in a balloon. They left from Maine in Double Eagle II and arrived in France 137 hours later. The crew’s goal was to complete their flight at Le Bourget airfield near Paris, where Charles Lindbergh completed his famous nonstop transatlantic flight in 1927. However, with ballast low and daylight fading, they decided to instead land in Normandy, where they were immediately surrounded by crowds who had been following the balloon's path. 

The Double Eagle II gondola, on display at the Udvar-Hazy Center, is our Maine "50 for 50" artifact. Personal artifacts from the Double Eagle II crew are on display at the Museum in DC.

60 years ago today, Gene Cernan became the third person, and second American, to walk in space on the Gemini IX-A mission. 

His planned EVA was highly ambitious, especially in view of zero American spacewalking experience since Ed White’s 20 minutes the year before. The primary task was to move to the back of the Gemini adapter section and strap into the Astronaut Maneuvering Unit (AMU), an Air Force backpack with control rockets fueled by hydrogen peroxide.

Because the resulting jets were hot, Cernan’s spacesuit had metal “pants,” an extra cover layer of woven steel on its lower half. He was to connect up to the AMU, disconnect his oxygen line from the spacecraft, and attached only by a tether, fly around the Gemini. Cernan would discover that this addition to his spacesuit greatly complicated his task and caused him to overheat to the point of his visor fogging over.

Visit the link in our bio to read the full blog about Cernan's nearly-disastrous spacewalk.

Federal Express (now known as FedEx) was founded in Little Rock, Arkansas, in 1971, and in 1972 it acquired two Dassault Falcon 20 cargo aircraft to start its fleet. In April 1973, the Dassault Cargo Falcon 20 on display at the Museum’s Steven F. Udvar-Hazy Center carried the first Federal Express air express package. This was a new milestone in the history of air transport in the United States and created a new category of airline, the exclusive air express carrier. Within a decade, no less than thirty-three were flying on the spokes of the Federal Express network.

Goodnight, MAVEN.

Mars once had an atmosphere thick enough to hold liquid water on its surface. MAVEN spent 11 years figuring out what happened to it.

Launched in 2013, it studied how the Sun slowly stripped the Martian atmosphere away over billions of years, turning a potentially habitable world into the cold, arid planet we see today. It discovered new types of auroras, watched a global dust storm loft water into space in real time, produced over 800 scientific publications, and it held the Solar System record for most data relayed from another planet in a single day. It was only supposed to last 1 year, it completed a decade more.

On December 6, MAVEN passed behind Mars and went silent. Telemetry before the pass showed everything working normally. When it emerged, NASA's Deep Space Network picked up nothing. A fragment of radio data revealed the spacecraft had entered safe mode and was spinning at an unusually high rate, likely from an orbital disruption. The batteries likely drained and the communications system lost power. An anomaly review board determined it could not be recovered. 

NASA confirmed today the mission has ended. Thank you to the MAVEN team for years of science. 

That'll do, MAVEN, that'll do.

This photo illustrates three phenomena that surprised me when I arrived on board the @iss. I didn’t realize before seeing it with my own eyes that the Earth’s atmosphere would be so visible in the night sky, seemingly even more obvious at nighttime than daytime, nor that it would be so colorful! These orange and green colors on the horizon are airglow, a faint emission of light caused by chemical reactions and interactions between UV radiation and gases in our atmosphere (unlike aurora, which is caused by solar wind particles interacting with Earth’s magnetic field, airglow is present everywhere, all of the time). I was also surprised to see how blue and well lit the Earth can be at night, when a bright Moon illuminates it (Moonglow!). This photo was taken on May 30, the day prior to the full Moon. I hope that this provides a bit more @nasaartemis Moon Joy for all of us!

"Look, the trees
are turning
their own bodies
into pillars

of light,
are giving off the rich
fragrance of cinnamon
and fulfillment,

the long tapers
of cattails
are bursting and floating away over
the blue shoulders

of the ponds,
and every pond,
no matter what its
name is, is

nameless now.
Every year
everything
I have ever learned

in my lifetime
leads back to this: the fires
and the black river of loss
whose other side

is salvation,
whose meaning
none of us will ever know.
To live in this world

you must be able
to do three things:
to love what is mortal;
to hold it

against your bones knowing
your own life depends on it;
and, when the time comes to let it
go,
to let it go."

-Mary Oliver

June 6th, 2025
One year ago today, my debut album was released into the world like a pigeon with a message from my heart!!! This album coming out changed everything for me. I loved, learned, lived, laughed, cried, danced, screamed, sang, danced harder, screamed louder, and lost & found myself over and over again. Thank you to everyone who helped to alchemize my dreaming into a reality. My partners in making music, Luka and Elvira, who are true masters as this craft, the creative team, so deeply filled with passion, the loyal and severely dedicated fans, my family whom I hope will always love and accept me anyways, my sorcerer magician managers, my trusting label for letting me share my vision, the ripped and worn clothes (or lack thereof), the inner and outer body moves, timeless choreography, the glamour, the pictures, the memories, the friends, the fame, the glory…... all of it!!!! Thank you. Pure and realized. I believe we are part of something veeeeerrrry special. If ‘Addison’ tells you anything, I hope it is to be true.. be honest.. turn your fears into your fantasy.. take control.. harness your divine energy.. and shamelessly experience freedom. Engraved in my heart, body, mind and soul to yours. 💓
